title: The Spinsterïŋ―s Sweet-Arts
content:
1
Milk for my sweet-arts, Bess! fur it mun be the time about now
When dolly cooms in froïŋ― the far-end close wiïŋ― her païŋ―ils froïŋ― the cow.
Eh! tha be new to the plaïŋ―ceïŋ―thouïŋ―rt gaïŋ―pinïŋ―ïŋ―doesnïŋ―t tha see
I calls ïŋ―em arter the fellers es once was sweet upoïŋ― me?

II

Naïŋ―y to be sewer it be past ïŋ―er time. What maïŋ―kes ïŋ―er sa laïŋ―te?
Goa to the laïŋ―ne at the back, anïŋ― looïŋ―k thruf Maddisonïŋ―s gaïŋ―te!

III

Sweet-arts! Molly belike may ïŋ―a lighted to-night upoïŋ― one.
Sweet-arts! thanks to the Lord that I niver not listenïŋ―d to noïŋ―n!
So I sits iïŋ― my oïŋ―n armchair wiïŋ― my oïŋ―n kettle theere oïŋ― the hob,
Anïŋ― Tommy the fust, anïŋ― Tommy the second, anïŋ― Steevie anïŋ― Rob.

IV

Rob, coom cop ïŋ―ere oïŋ― my knee. Thou sees that iïŋ― spite oïŋ― the men
I ïŋ―a kepïŋ― thruf thick anïŋ― thin my two ïŋ―oonderd a-year to mysen;
Yis! thaw tha callïŋ―d me es pretty es ony lass iïŋ― the Shere;
Anïŋ― thou be es pretty a Tabby, but Robby I seed thruf ya theere.

V

Feyther ïŋ―ud saïŋ―y I wur ugly es sin, anïŋ― I beïŋ―nt not vaïŋ―in,
But I niver wur downright hugly, thaw soom ïŋ―ud ïŋ―a thowt ma plaïŋ―in,
Anïŋ― I wasnïŋ―t sa plaïŋ―in iïŋ― pink ribbons, ye said I wur pretty iïŋ― pinks,
Anïŋ― I liked to ïŋ―ear it I did, but I brunt sich a fool as ye thinks;
Ye was stroïŋ―kin ma down wiïŋ― the ïŋ―air, as I be a-stroïŋ―kin oïŋ― you,
But whiniver I looïŋ―ked iïŋ― the glass I wur sewer that it couldnïŋ―t be true;
Niver wur pretty, not I, but ye knawïŋ―d it wur pleasant to ïŋ―ear,
Thaw it warnïŋ―t not me es wur pretty, but my two ïŋ―oonderd a-year.

VI

Dïŋ―ya mind the murninïŋ― when we was a-walkinïŋ― togither, anïŋ― stood
By the claïŋ―yïŋ―d-oop pond, that the foalk be sa scared at, iïŋ― Gigglesby wood,
Wheer the poor wench drowndid hersen, black Sal, esïŋ―ed been disgraïŋ―ced?
Anïŋ― I feelïŋ―d thy arm es I stood wur a-creeïŋ―pin about my waïŋ―ist;
Anïŋ― me es wur allus afearïŋ―d of a manïŋ―s gittinïŋ― over fond,
I sidled awaïŋ―y anïŋ― awaïŋ―y till I plumpt foot fust iïŋ― the pond;
And, Robby, I niver ïŋ―a liked tha sa well, as I did that daïŋ―y,
Fur tha joompt in thysen, anïŋ― tha hoickt my feet wiïŋ― a flop froïŋ― the claïŋ―y.
Ay, stick oop thy back, anïŋ― set oop thy taïŋ―il, tha may gie ma a kiss,
Fur I walkïŋ―d wiïŋ― tha all the way hoam anïŋ― wur niver sa nigh saïŋ―yinïŋ― Yis.
But wa boath was iïŋ― sich a clat we was shaïŋ―med to cross Gigglesby Greeïŋ―n,
Fur a cat may looïŋ―k at a king thou knaws but the cat mun be clean.
Sa we boïŋ―th on us kep out oïŋ― sight oïŋ― the winders oïŋ― Gigglesby Hinnïŋ―
Naïŋ―y, but the claws oïŋ― tha! quiet! they pricks clean thruf to the skinïŋ―
Anïŋ― wa boïŋ―th slinkt ïŋ―oïŋ―m by the brokken shed iïŋ― the laïŋ―ne at the back,
Wheer the poodle runnïŋ―d at tha once, anïŋ― thou runnïŋ―d oop oïŋ― the thack;
Anïŋ― tha squeedgïŋ―d my ïŋ―and iïŋ― the shed, fur theere we was forced to ïŋ―ide,
Fur I seed that Steevie wur coominïŋ―, and one oïŋ― the Tommies beside.

VII

Theere now, what art ïŋ―a mewin at, Steevie? for owt I can tellïŋ―
Robby wur fust to be sewer, or I mowt ïŋ―a liked tha as well.

VIII

But, Robby, I thowt oïŋ― tha all the while I wur chaïŋ―nginïŋ― my gown,
Anïŋ― I thowt shall I chaïŋ―nge my staïŋ―te? but, O Lord, upoïŋ― coominïŋ― downïŋ―
My bran-new carpet es fresh es a midder oïŋ― flowers iïŋ― Maïŋ―yïŋ―
Why ïŋ―ednïŋ―t tha wiped thy shoes? it wur clatted all ower wiïŋ― claïŋ―y.
Anïŋ― I could ïŋ―a cried ammost, fur I seed that it couldnïŋ―t be,
Anïŋ― Robby I gied tha a raïŋ―tin that sattled thy coortin oïŋ― me.
Anïŋ― Molly anïŋ― me was agreed, as we was a-cleaninïŋ― the floor,
That a man be a durty thing anïŋ― a trouble anïŋ― plague wiïŋ― indoor.
But I rued it arter a bit, fur I stuck to tha moor na the rest,
But I couldnïŋ―t ïŋ―a lived wiïŋ― a man anïŋ― I knaws it be all fur the best.

IX

Naïŋ―yïŋ―let ma stroïŋ―k tha down till I maïŋ―kes tha es smooth es silk,
But if I ïŋ―ed married tha, Robby, thouïŋ―d not ïŋ―a been worth thy milk,
Thouïŋ―d niver ïŋ―a cotchïŋ―d ony mice but ïŋ―a left me the work to do,
And ïŋ―a taïŋ―en to the bottle beside, so es all that I ïŋ―ears be true;
But I loovs tha to maïŋ―ke thysen ïŋ―appy, anïŋ― soa purr awaïŋ―y, my dear,
Thou ïŋ―ed wellnigh purrïŋ―d ma awaïŋ―y froïŋ― my oïŋ―n two ïŋ―oonderd a-year.

X

Sweïŋ―rin agean, you Toms, as ye used to do twelve year sinïŋ―!
Ye niver ïŋ―eïŋ―rd Steevie swear ïŋ―cepïŋ― it wur at a dog coominïŋ― in,
Anïŋ― boath oïŋ― ye mun be fools to be hallus a-shawinïŋ― your claws,
Fur I niver cared nothink for neitherïŋ―anïŋ― one oïŋ― ye deïŋ―d ye knaws!
Coom give hoïŋ―ver then, weant ye? I warrant ye soom fine daïŋ―yïŋ―
Theere, dig downïŋ―I shall hew to gie one or tother awaïŋ―y.
Canïŋ―t ye taïŋ―ke pattern by Steevie? ye shant hew a drop froïŋ― the païŋ―il.
Steevie be right good manners bang thruf to the tip oïŋ― the taïŋ―il.

Xl.
Robby, git down wiïŋ―tha, wilt tha? let Steevie coom oop oïŋ― my knee.
Steevie, my lad, thou ïŋ―ed very nigh been the Steevie fur me!
Robby wur fust to be sewer, ïŋ―e wur burn anïŋ― bred iïŋ― the ïŋ―ouse,
But thou be es ïŋ―ansom a tabby es iver patted a mouse.

XII

Anïŋ― I beïŋ―nt not vaïŋ―in, but I knaws I ïŋ―ed led tha a quieter life
Nor her wiïŋ― the hepitaph yonder! ïŋ―A faïŋ―ithfnl anïŋ― loovinïŋ― wife!ïŋ―
Anïŋ― ïŋ―cos oïŋ― thy farm by the beck, anïŋ― thy windmill oop oïŋ― the croft,
Tha thowt tha would marry ma, did tha? but that wur a bit ower soft,
Thaw thou was es soïŋ―ber es daïŋ―y, wiïŋ― a niced red faïŋ―ce, anïŋ― es cleïŋ―n
Es a shillinïŋ― fresh froïŋ― the mint wiïŋ― a bran-new ïŋ―eïŋ―d oïŋ― the Queeïŋ―n,
Anïŋ― thy farminïŋ― es cleïŋ―n es thysenïŋ―, fur, Steevie, tha kepïŋ― it sa neïŋ―t
That I niver not spied sa much es a poppy along wiïŋ― the wheïŋ―t,
Anïŋ― the wool of a thistle a-flyinïŋ― anïŋ― seeïŋ―dinïŋ― tha haïŋ―ted to see;
ïŋ―Twur es bad es a battle-twig1 ïŋ―ere iïŋ― my oïŋ―n blue chaumber to me.
Ay, roob thy whiskers ageïŋ―n ma, fur I could ïŋ―a taïŋ―en to tha well,
But fur thy bairns, poor Steevie, a bouncinïŋ― boy anïŋ― a gell.

XIII

Anïŋ― thou was es fond oïŋ― thy bairns es I be mysen oïŋ― my cats,
But I niver not wishïŋ―d fur childer, I hevnïŋ―t naw likinïŋ― fur brats;
Pretty anew when ya dresses ïŋ―em oop, anïŋ― they goïŋ―s fur a walk,
Or sits wiïŋ― their ïŋ―ands afoor ïŋ―em, anïŋ― doesnïŋ―t not ïŋ―inder the talk!
But their bottles oïŋ― pap, anïŋ― their mucky bibs, anïŋ― the clats anïŋ― the clouts,
Anïŋ― their mashinïŋ― their toys to pieïŋ―ces anïŋ― maïŋ―kinïŋ― ma deaf wiïŋ― their shouts,
Anïŋ― hallus a-joompinïŋ― about ma as if they was set upoïŋ― springs,
Anïŋ― a haxinïŋ― ma hawkard questions, anïŋ― saïŋ―yinïŋ― ondecent things,
Altïŋ― a-callinïŋ― ma ïŋ―huglyïŋ― mayhap to my faïŋ―ce, or a teïŋ―rinïŋ― my gownïŋ―
Dear! dear! dear! I mun part them Tommiesïŋ―Steevie git down.

XIV

Ye be wuss nor the men-tommies, you. I tellïŋ―d ya, na moor oïŋ― that!
Tom, lig theere oïŋ― the cushion, anïŋ― tother Tom ïŋ―ere oïŋ― the mat.

XV

Theere! I haïŋ― masterïŋ―d them! Hed I married the Tommiesïŋ―O Lord,
To loove anïŋ― obaïŋ―y the Tommies! I couldnïŋ―t ïŋ―a stuck by my word.
To be horderïŋ―d about, anïŋ― waïŋ―ked, when Molly ïŋ―d put out the light,
By a man coominïŋ― in wiïŋ― a hiccup at ony hour oïŋ― the night!
Anïŋ― the taïŋ―ble staïŋ―inïŋ―d wiïŋ― ïŋ―is aïŋ―le, anïŋ― the mud oïŋ― ïŋ―is boots oïŋ― the stairs,
Anïŋ― the stink oïŋ― ïŋ―is pipe iïŋ― the ïŋ―ouse, anïŋ― the mark oïŋ― ïŋ―is ïŋ―eïŋ―d oïŋ― the chairs!
Anïŋ― noun oïŋ― my four sweet-arts ïŋ―ud ïŋ―a let me ïŋ―a led my oïŋ―n waïŋ―y,
Sa I likes ïŋ―em best wiïŋ― taïŋ―ils when they ïŋ―evnïŋ―t a word to saïŋ―y.

XVII

Anïŋ― I sits iïŋ― my oïŋ―n little parlour, anïŋ― sarved by my oïŋ―n little lass,
Wiïŋ― my oïŋ―n little garden outside, anïŋ― my oïŋ―n bed oïŋ― sparrow-grass,
Anïŋ― my oïŋ―n door-poorch wi the woodbine anïŋ― jessmine a-dressinïŋ― it greeïŋ―n,
Anïŋ― my oïŋ―n fine Jackman iïŋ― purple a roïŋ―binïŋ― the ïŋ―ouse like a Queeïŋ―n.

XVII

Anïŋ― the little gells bobs to ma hoffens es I be abroad iïŋ― the laïŋ―nes,
When I goïŋ―s fur to coomfut the poor es be down wiïŋ― their haïŋ―ches anïŋ― their païŋ―ins:
Anïŋ― a haïŋ―f-pot oïŋ― jam, or a mossel oïŋ― meïŋ―t when it beïŋ―nt too dear,
They maïŋ―kes ma a graïŋ―ter Laïŋ―dy nor ïŋ―er iïŋ― the mansion theer,
Hes ïŋ―es hallus to hax of a man how much to spare or to spend;
Anïŋ― a spinster I be anïŋ― I will be, if soïŋ― pleïŋ―se God, to the hend.

XVIII

Mew! mew!ïŋ―Bess wiïŋ― the milk! what ha maïŋ―de our Molly sa laïŋ―te?
It should ïŋ―a been ïŋ―ere by seven, anïŋ― theereïŋ―it be strikinïŋ― heightïŋ―
ïŋ―Cushie wur craïŋ―zed furïŋ―er caufïŋ― wellïŋ―I ïŋ―eïŋ―rd ïŋ―er a maïŋ―kinïŋ― ïŋ―er moïŋ―n,
Anïŋ― I thowt to mysen ïŋ―thank God that I hevnïŋ―t naw cauf oïŋ― my oïŋ―n.ïŋ―
Theere!
Set it down!
Now Robby!
You Tommies shall waïŋ―it to-night
Till Robby anïŋ― Steevie ïŋ―es ïŋ―ed their lapïŋ―anïŋ― it sarves ye right.
meaning:
Alfred Lord Tennyson's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "The Spinsterïŋ―s Sweet-Arts"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
